active_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.linprog for optimal radius computation
active_paradigms.description: Replace the iterative compute_max_radii function with linear programming. For fixed positions, finding optimal radii is a linear program: maximize sum of radii subject to r_i + r_j ≤ distance_ij for all pairs, and r_i ≤ boundary_distance_i. Build constraint matrix A_ub where each row represents a pairwise constraint, use bounds for boundary constraints, and call linprog with method='highs'. This guarantees mathematically optimal radii for any position configuration, unlike the heuristic iterative approach that may converge to suboptimal local solutions.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Primary metric sum_radii - LP finds optimal radii for given positions, improving the objective function accuracy in the differential_evolution outer loop
active_paradigms.cautions: Handle the large constraint matrix (325 pairwise constraints for 26 circles). Use method='highs' for efficiency. Ensure positions are clipped to valid range before building constraints. Return zeros if LP fails.

active_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.spatial.Voronoi with Lloyd's algorithm for geometric relaxation
active_paradigms.description: Apply Voronoi-based geometric construction without optimization. Initialize positions in a hexagonal-like pattern, then iterate: (1) Compute Voronoi diagram of current centers with boundary mirror points, (2) Set each circle's radius to minimum distance to its Voronoi cell edges and square boundaries, (3) Move each center toward the centroid of its Voronoi cell. This Lloyd relaxation naturally maximizes distances between circles. After convergence, compute final radii. This is pure geometry, no optimization function calls.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum_radii through geometric spacing optimization - Voronoi cells naturally define regions where each circle can expand
active_paradigms.cautions: Handle unbounded Voronoi regions (vertices at infinity) by clipping to square. Add mirror points outside boundaries to constrain Voronoi cells. Use finite vertices only for centroid computation. May need 50-100 iterations for convergence.

active_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.integrate.solve_ivp for physics-based dynamics simulation
active_paradigms.description: Model circles as soft repelling particles and simulate dynamics to equilibrium. Define ODE system where each circle experiences: (1) Repulsive forces from nearby circles proportional to overlap, (2) Restoring forces from boundaries. Use solve_ivp with RK45 method to integrate from random initial positions until equilibrium. The final positions naturally maximize inter-circle distances. Then compute radii as minimum distances to neighbors and boundaries. This is fundamentally different from optimization - it's physics simulation.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum_radii through natural equilibrium of repulsive dynamics - circles spread to maximize their territories
active_paradigms.cautions: Use appropriate time span and step size. Add damping to ensure convergence. Clip final positions to [0.01, 0.99]. The ODE should model soft-sphere repulsion with force ~1/distance². Set tolerances rtol=1e-6, atol=1e-9 for accuracy.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.root to solve contact constraint equations for exact positioning
tried_paradigms.description: Formulate circle packing as a system of nonlinear equations where circles touch their neighbors and/or boundaries. Define contact constraints: for touching circles i and j, ||c_i - c_j|| = r_i + r_j. For circles touching walls, c_i - r_i = 0 or c_i + r_i = 1. Use scipy.optimize.root to solve this system starting from a good initial guess (hexagonal pattern). This finds positions that satisfy geometric constraints exactly, unlike optimization which searches for maxima. The solution gives both positions and radii that are locally optimal for the specified contact graph.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: primary evaluator score through exact constraint satisfaction
tried_paradigms.cautions: The contact graph must be well-defined (which circles touch which). Use a good initial guess close to the solution. May need to try different contact patterns. scipy.optimize.root is equation solving, not optimization - fundamentally different from failed attempts.

tried_paradigms.idea: Deterministic hexagonal lattice construction with analytical radius computation
tried_paradigms.description: Construct a packing directly using hexagonal geometry without optimization. Determine optimal row arrangement for 26 circles (e.g., 4+5+4+5+4+4 or 5+6+5+6+4). Compute positions analytically: horizontal spacing s, vertical spacing s*sqrt(3)/2, with alternating row offsets of s/2. For each circle, compute radius directly as min(distance to nearest boundary, half distance to nearest neighbor). This eliminates the non-smooth iterative radius computation entirely. Use numpy for array operations to place circles and compute radii in closed form.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum of radii by maximizing hexagonal packing efficiency within square boundaries
tried_paradigms.cautions: Test multiple row arrangements to find best fit. Edge circles will have smaller radii computed from boundary distance. Ensure spacing is chosen so all circles fit within [0,1] with computed radii.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.minimize with trust-constr for joint position-radius optimization
tried_paradigms.description: Fundamentally change the optimization by jointly optimizing all 78 variables (positions AND radii) with explicit non-linear constraints. Define boundary constraints: r_i ≤ x_i, r_i ≤ y_i, r_i ≤ 1-x_i, r_i ≤ 1-y_i for each circle. Define non-overlap constraints: ||c_i - c_j||² ≥ (r_i + r_j)² for all pairs. Initialize with hexagonal pattern positions and analytically computed radii. The trust-constr method handles these non-linear constraints directly, unlike the current approach which embeds them in a non-smooth objective.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum of radii with guaranteed constraint satisfaction through explicit constraints
tried_paradigms.cautions: Formulate constraints as scipy.optimize.NonlinearConstraint with proper jacobians. Use 'l-bfgs-b' bounds for positions [0.05, 0.95] and radii [0.01, 0.5]. Multiple random starts from hexagonal perturbations recommended.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.basinhopping with structured initialization and local refinement
tried_paradigms.description: Apply basin-hopping which uses a fundamentally different search paradigm: random perturbations followed by local minimization, hopping between basins of attraction. Unlike differential_evolution's population-based evolution, basin-hopping can escape local optima by accepting uphill moves with Metropolis criterion. Initialize with hexagonal lattice positions. Use L-BFGS-B as local minimizer with bounds [0.02, 0.98] for positions. The key difference is how it explores the landscape - by hopping between local minima rather than evolving a population.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum of radii through global landscape exploration with local refinement
tried_paradigms.cautions: Set niter=300-500 for sufficient exploration. Tune take_step parameter for appropriate perturbation size (~0.1-0.2). Use temperature parameter to control uphill move acceptance. The iterative radius computation still creates non-smoothness but basin-hopping is more robust to this.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.dual_annealing for global optimization with embedded radius computation
tried_paradigms.description: Replace differential_evolution with scipy.optimize.dual_annealing, which combines simulated annealing for global search with local minimization. The objective function computes maximum radii via iterative constraint propagation (as in current code). Dual annealing is better at escaping local minima in high-dimensional non-convex landscapes. Use default parameters with increased maxiter=2000. The key advantage over differential_evolution is the acceptance of worse solutions during search, enabling broader exploration of the solution space.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum of radii through better global exploration of position space
tried_paradigms.cautions: Set seed for reproducibility, use bounds [(0.05,0.95)]*52, ensure radius computation is efficient (limit iterations), clip final centers to valid range

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.basinhopping with L-BFGS-B local minimizer for position optimization
tried_paradigms.description: Apply scipy.optimize.basinhopping which performs random perturbations followed by local minimization, accepting moves based on Metropolis criterion. Use L-BFGS-B as the local minimizer with bounds on positions. The objective function computes radii iteratively from positions. Basin-hopping is particularly effective for problems with many local minima (like circle packing). Configure with niter=500, T=1.0 for temperature parameter, and stepsize=0.1. Initialize with a good starting point (e.g., hexagonal-like arrangement).
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum of radii by escaping local minima through stochastic jumps
tried_paradigms.cautions: Provide explicit bounds via minimizer_kwargs, set random seed, the take_step parameter can customize step distribution, ensure objective returns negative sum for minimization

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.minimize with trust-constr method and explicit nonlinear constraints
tried_paradigms.description: Formulate the problem as a direct constrained optimization with 78 variables (52 positions + 26 radii). Use scipy.optimize.minimize with method='trust-constr' which handles nonlinear constraints properly. Define constraints: (1) boundary constraints: x_i - r_i >= 0, x_i + r_i <= 1, etc. (2) overlap constraints: ||c_i - c_j|| >= r_i + r_j for all pairs. The objective is to maximize sum of radii (minimize negative sum). Trust-constr is designed for problems with many nonlinear constraints and uses interior-point methods with trust regions.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: joint optimization of positions and radii with exact constraint handling
tried_paradigms.cautions: NonlinearConstraint objects needed for overlap constraints, use sparse Jacobian if possible, start from feasible initial point (current solution), set appropriate bounds for all 78 variables, may need ftol and gtol adjustments for convergence

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.dual_annealing for global position optimization
tried_paradigms.description: Replace differential_evolution with dual_annealing which combines simulated annealing with local search. dual_annealing uses a temperature-based acceptance criterion that allows accepting worse solutions temporarily to escape local minima - fundamentally different from DE's evolutionary approach. Define objective function that computes negative sum of radii for given positions using iterative radius computation. Call dual_annealing(objective, bounds=[(0.05,0.95)]*52, maxiter=3000, seed=42). The simulated annealing mechanism explores the search space differently, potentially finding configurations DE misses.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Find better global positions using simulated annealing exploration strategy that can escape local minima
tried_paradigms.cautions: dual_annealing may require more function evaluations than DE. Set maxiter sufficiently high (3000+) for thorough exploration. Use default local search options (L-BFGS-B) for final refinement. The objective function must handle numerical edge cases gracefully. May need to experiment with initial_temp and restart_temp_ratio parameters.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.shgo for global optimization with simplicial homology
tried_paradigms.description: Use shgo (Simplicial Homology Global Optimization) which finds global minima using simplicial homology theory - a fundamentally different approach from evolutionary algorithms. shgo systematically explores the search space by constructing simplicial complexes and identifying promising regions for local refinement. Call shgo(objective, bounds=[(0.05,0.95)]*52, minimizer_kwargs={'method':'L-BFGS-B'}, sampling_method='sobol', n=300, iters=5). This provides theoretical guarantees about finding global minima under certain conditions.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Find global optimum through systematic search space exploration using topological methods
tried_paradigms.cautions: shgo can be computationally expensive for high dimensions (52 variables). Use 'sobol' sampling for space-filling efficiency. Set appropriate n (sampling points) and iters for convergence. The minimizer_kwargs should specify bounds for local refinement. May need to increase maxiter if convergence is slow.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.minimize with SLSQP method and structured initialization
tried_paradigms.description: Use SLSQP (Sequential Least Squares Programming) which handles nonlinear constraints through Lagrange multipliers - different from trust-constr's interior point approach. SLSQP constructs quadratic subproblems and uses Lagrange multiplier updates. Initialize with hexagonal lattice positions (not random) to provide better starting points. Run minimize with method='SLSQP' from the structured initialization, with bounds on positions. SLSQP's different constraint handling mechanism may find solutions trust-constr misses.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Find optimum with different constraint handling mechanism, starting from better initial positions
tried_paradigms.cautions: SLSQP requires bounds to be specified explicitly. Use the bounds parameter for position constraints (0.05 to 0.95). The objective computes radii from positions, so constraints are implicit. Set maxiter sufficiently high (1000+). SLSQP may converge to local minima, so the structured initialization is critical.
